attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with sudden onset of left calf pain and swelling. She has a history of two miscarriages in the second trimester over the past three years. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 120/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Her left calf is swollen, warm, and tender to touch. Doppler ultrasound confirms a deep vein thrombosis in the left popliteal vein.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following should be tested to help establish the underlying diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features suggestive of antiphospholipid syndrome (APS), including unprovoked deep vein thrombosis in a young patient and a history of recurrent second-trimester miscarriages. The most appropriate antibody to test in this scenario is anti-cardiolipin antibodies, which are one of the key diagnostic markers for APS.

Anti Xa levels are used to monitor anticoagulation therapy, particularly with low molecular weight heparins and direct oral anticoagulants. These are not diagnostic antibodies for any underlying thrombophilic condition and would not be appropriate for establishing a diagnosis in this case.

Anti-centromere antibodies are associated with limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (lcSSc) and are particularly linked to pulmonary arterial hypertension and digital ulceration. This patient's presentation is not consistent with systemic sclerosis, making these antibodies inappropriate for this clinical scenario.

Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are highly specific for rheumatoid arthritis. This patient's presentation is not consistent with rheumatoid arthritis, so testing for these antibodies would not be appropriate.

Anti-double stranded DNA antibodies are highly specific for systemic lupus erythematosus. While lupus can be associated with APS, these antibodies are not directly related to the diagnosis of APS and would not be the most appropriate initial test in this scenario.

Antiphospholipid syndrome is characterised by the presence of antiphospholipid antibodies (including anti-cardiolipin, anti-β2 glycoprotein I, and lupus anticoagulant) in association with thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity. The diagnosis requires both clinical criteria (thrombosis or specific pregnancy complications) and laboratory criteria (presence of antiphospholipid antibodies on two or more occasions at least 12 weeks apart). In this case, testing for anti-cardiolipin antibodies would be an appropriate first step in establishing the diagnosis of APS, given the patient's presentation with unprovoked thrombosis and history of recurrent miscarriages.
learningPoints: Anti-cardiolipin and anti-β2 glycoprotein I antibodies are key diagnostic markers for antiphospholipid syndrome.
Antiphospholipid syndrome can present with recurrent thrombosis and pregnancy complications.
Testing for antiphospholipid antibodies should be considered in young patients with unprovoked thrombosis.
references: Knight JS, Branch DW, Ortel TL. Antiphospholipid syndrome: advances in diagnosis, pathogenesis, and management. BMJ. 2023;380:e069717. Published 2023 Feb 27. doi:10.1136/bmj-2021-069717
